text:
Border studies focus on the multiple mechanisms of boundary formation, their interpretation and their collapse. During the last two decades border studies have become a very dynamic and highly popular area of research in the academe, largely because of the dynamics of globalization and the lingering legacy of the Cold War. Common perception is that borders refer to the physical boundaries that divide sovereign states or nation states or even other entities like the European Union. The dynamics of globalization have transformed the nature of the borders into more dynamic and multifaceted entities through the increasing permeability to the flow of capital, ideas and people.

BOUNDARIES, HAMPSHIRE, AND THE CONCEPT OF BORDERS IN AN AGE OF GLOBALIZATION The boundaries that separate territorial entities no longer function in the same way as their more physical cousins of old. Global political and economic structures are changing the terms of their interaction and increasing their interdependence. At the same time, however, the competitive and unsettling consequences of globalization have seen borders being employed as a means of protection against the feared effects of globalization through the assertion of exclusionist ideologies of ethnic and national identity. Increasingly, the study of borders has become a major area of interest in the social sciences in the United States and, more recently, in Europe. The effect of globalization has been to shift the focus of attention from maps to the processes that maintain on a daily basis their boundary indicators, physical or symbolic, and to recognize the major role that borders have to play in ensuring the maintenance of security, economic development, and ethnic and national identity in an increasingly integrated if often dysfunctional region such as Europe. This volume is concerned with an empirical exploration of a number of European "case studies" of border interaction in order to shed light on the processes by which people and organizations adjust to the contradictions inherent in the borders of an integrating and simultaneously exclusionist region.
